Title: MOF: Budi95 display error due to technical glitch, quota stays at 200 litres
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Finance Ministry (MOF) has clarified that the display of a 150-litre monthly Budi95 limit seen by some users during system maintenance at 11pm last night was due to a technical error.
It said the technical fault has been resolved.
"The Budi95 monthly quota remains at 200 litres for eligible users, including for June 2026," the MOF said in a statement today.
The ministry said it remains committed to the smooth and orderly rollout of Budi95, ensuring subsidies are delivered to eligible recipients.
"Any confusion arising from the error is deeply regretted," it said.
Budi95, or the Budi Madani RON95 initiative, is a targeted government fuel subsidy programme offering RON95 petrol at a fixed subsidised rate of
RM1.99 per litre
.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Curfew imposed in New Jersey city following ICE protest violence
Article:
NEW YORK: The mayor of a New Jersey city declared a curfew Sunday around a migrant detention centre after another night of clashes between police and protesters opposed to President Donald Trump's immigration policies.
"To ensure the safety and well-being of all residents, a mandatory curfew for a half-mile area surrounding Delaney Hall is being implemented, effective immediately," Newark Mayor Ras Baraka said in a statement.
Access to the Delaney Hall facility, which is operated by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), will be prohibited from 9pm to 6am until further notice, the mayor said.
The decision followed another night of tensions outside the 1,000-bed privately operated facility, which has been the focus of protests for several days.
After a day of largely peaceful demonstrations on Saturday, including a counter-protest in support of ICE, some protesters attempted to breach a police barricade, prompting police to use tear gas.
Mikie Sherrill, New Jersey's Democratic governor, condemned the violence.
"I don't know why these individuals attacked or what they are trying to do, but I refuse to let these dangerous acts undermine New Jersey's commitment to public safety," Sherrill said on X.
On Friday, authorities sought to ease tensions by transferring security responsibilities from ICE to state police and setting up designated protest areas. The measures, however, failed to prevent further nighttime clashes.
The protests were triggered by a hunger and work strike launched by detainees at Delaney Hall over their living conditions.
The strike has drawn support from a number of Democratic lawmakers.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IT consultant loses RM1.49mil in crypto investment scam
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A 63-year-old information technology consultant lost RM1.49 million after falling victim to an online cryptocurrency investment scam promoted through Facebook.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the victim lodged a report with the southwest district police headquarters' commercial crime investigation department on May 30 after realising he had been duped by a fraudulent investment scheme known as "Crypto Kakaue."
"The victim claimed he came across a cryptocurrency investment advertisement on Facebook in December 2023 and expressed interest in joining the scheme.
"After clicking on the link in the advertisement, he was contacted by a suspect via WhatsApp, who offered an investment opportunity involving the trading of USDT cryptocurrency through a platform known as 'Crypto Kakaue'," he said in a statement.
Azizee said the victim was later added to a WhatsApp group called "Saham Mececah Hati Orang", where members were provided with information about the investment and promised lucrative returns.
He said the victim was instructed to download an investment application known as "KAKAUE" and conduct cryptocurrency purchase transactions through individuals who communicated with him via WhatsApp.
Between March and September 2024, the victim made nine money transfers totalling RM1,494,410 to six bank accounts belonging to companies specified by the suspects.
"However, after making the payments, the victim discovered that he was unable to withdraw or transfer either the purported profits or his investment capital displayed in the application to his personal bank account.
"Realising he had been deceived, the victim lodged a police report for further investigation," he added.
Azizee said the case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar congratulates King on his birthday
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has extended his congratulations to His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, in conjunction with His Majesty's official birthday celebration.
Anwar, on behalf of the Madani government and the people, prayed that His Majesty, the Queen, Raja Zarith Sofiah, and all members of the royal family be blessed with continued good health and remain under the protection and grace of Allah.
"His Majesty will continue to reign justly and fairly, and remain at the highest position under the Federal Constitution, in line with the role of the monarchy as protector of Islam and a symbol of unity and harmony among Malaysia's multiracial society," he said.
"The people and I will continue to pledge unwavering loyalty to the constitutional monarchy.
"Dirgahayu Tuanku!" he said in a Facebook post today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US takes step to halt Nvidia AI chip shipments to Chinese firms outside China
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US Department of Commerce on Sunday moved to close a potential loophole that may have led companies to export the world's most advanced chips — such as Nvidia's most sophisticated Blackwell processors — to subsidiaries of Chinese companies located outside China.
The unexpected guidance suggests that the United States' best AI chips may have been making their way to subsidiaries of Chinese AI firms based in places such as Malaysia despite broader US efforts to deny Chinese firms access to semiconductors needed to develop critical AI capabilities.
The new guidance was posted on the Commerce Department's website on Sunday after a paper about the loophole circulated in Washington, according to people familiar with the matter.
The paper, a copy of which was seen by Reuters, said "the floodgates have quietly opened". Dated Friday, the paper does not list any author.
It is unclear how many of the chips have been exported in the year that the Trump administration left the door open.
One chip industry source with deep supply-chain knowledge estimated the number was in the hundreds of thousands.
In the unusual weekend guidance, the department's Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) said it would enforce licence requirements for advanced chips to entities headquartered in China when those entities were located outside China.
"BIS issued guidance clarifying export licence requirements that have been in place since 2023," a bureau spokesperson said.
"BIS will continue to enforce export controls rigorously to safeguard critical American technology."
The new guidance does not change anything for Nvidia, a company official said, adding that it could not ship the chips because the Commerce Department had clearly imposed a licence requirement on Nvidia in a letter.
AMD, another major producer of sought-after AI chips, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The Commerce Department created the opening when it announced in May 2025 that it would not be enforcing the AI Diffusion rule issued in the final days of the Biden administration.
The rule had licensing requirements governing global access to AI chips.
Former State Department official Chris McGuire said in a social media post on Sunday that the loophole allowed the overseas subsidiaries of Chinese companies to buy Nvidia Blackwell chips without a licence.
"This is a HUGE problem," he said.
"Chinese companies have been buying these chips, very likely at scale," McGuire said. He added that while the guidance closes one loophole, it leaves another open.
That remaining loophole removes the requirement for Taiwan-based TSMC and other foundries to conduct additional due diligence to ensure that high-end AI chips they manufacture are not intended for Chinese front companies. He said the issue was not addressed by the new guidance.
A spokesperson for TSMC declined comment.
In addition, the new guidance does not require data centres to stop using the chips or cut off servicing of advanced computing equipment such as servers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Macron: Nothing justifies Israel's escalation in Lebanon
Article:
PARIS: French President Emmanuel Macron on Sunday said that "nothing justifies the major escalation under way in south Lebanon" where Israeli forces have launched a new offensive against Hizbollah.
In a message on X after speaking with regional leaders, Macron said it was "essential" for an agreement to be reached quickly between the United States and Iran.
Macron held talks with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, Oman's Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, United Arab Emirates President Mohammed bin Zayed, and his Egyptian counterpart Abdel Fattah al-Sisi.
At France's request, the UN Security Council will hold an emergency meeting on Monday  after the Israeli army, which is expanding its operations in Lebanon, seized the medieval Beaufort castle in the country's south.
"France will continue to support the Lebanese authorities in their efforts to restore the sovereignty of the state and the country's territorial integrity," Macron wrote.
Regional stability "must begin with Lebanon, where it is urgent that all weapons fall silent, for good."
More broadly, he said a ceasefire must be a "priority" between the United States and Iran, and called for the immediate reopening of the Strait of Hormuz "without any conditions and in accordance with international law."
"Talks must then continue in order to reach a comprehensive and robust agreement on other issues, particularly the nuclear and ballistic programmes and regional stability," he added.
"France stands ready to fully play its part by helping to restore maritime traffic through the independent multinational mission set up with the United Kingdom," he said.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil prices rise more than 2pct as Israel steps up incursion into Lebanon
Article:
BEIJING: Oil prices rose more than two per cent in early trading on Monday after Israel ordered troops to move further into Lebanon in the battle with the Iranian-backed Hezbollah militant group, despite a ceasefire announced more than six weeks ago.
US crude futures rose US$2.37, or 2.71 per cent, to US$89.73 a barrel as of 0028 GMT.
Brent futures rose US$2.16, or 2.37 per cent, to US$93.28 a barrel.
The stepped-up fighting, coming just after the US hosted Israeli-Lebanon peace talks in Washington on Friday, dimmed expectations that the US and Iran could soon announce an extension to their ceasefire agreement, which had driven Brent and WTI to settle up 1.80 per cent and 1.70 per cent, respectively, on Friday.
The Israel-Lebanon conflict has been the broadest spillover of the Iran war. It started on March 2 when Hezbollah began firing rockets and drones across the border into Israel to back its ally Iran.
The two sides reached a ceasefire in mid-April but have continued to trade fire.
US President Donald Trump said on Friday that he would soon decide on a proposed deal to extend a ceasefire with Iran announced in early April, giving negotiators more time to seek a permanent end to the conflict and find a solution to the underlying dispute over Iran's nuclear programme.
Israel would be key to any such deal, and Iran has also said repeatedly that Hezbollah must be included.
Meanwhile, concerns are rising about mines in the key oil and gas shipping lane, the Strait of Hormuz, IG analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note.
That could slow the process of reopening the strait and mean that relief comes more slowly for the oil market even after it is reopened.
"Even if an agreement is reached, it won't deliver a flood of supply," Sycamore said.
An Axios reporter said on X on Friday that Iran had dropped more mines in the strait earlier in the week, shortly after US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said that attempts to lay more mines would be a violation of the ceasefire.
Hormuz is a conduit for about a fifth of global oil and gas flows and Iran has effectively closed it since the conflict began with US and Israeli strikes in February.
Concerns over supply outweighed lacklustre economic data from China over the weekend, which showed stalling factory activity.
This added to concerns that the world's second-largest economy is losing momentum, weighed down by a contraction in exports and cost pressures.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New Structure MyKad rollout this month marks new chapter in ID card evolution
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: After more than two decades, Malaysia's national identity card is entering a new phase with the rollout of the New Structure MyKad this month, featuring enhanced security measures to protect personal identities and data.
The phased rollout, beginning this month, marks the latest effort to enhance the security of the MyKad, first introduced on Sept 5, 2001, to ensure it remains resilient against cyber threats and document forgery.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail previously said the next-generation MyKad is intended to strengthen the national identity system through the adoption of improved technology and security features.
National Registration Department (NRD) director-general Datuk Badrul Hisham Alias said the New Structure MyKad will feature 53 security elements, compared with 23 in the current version.
Among the enhancements are QR codes for verification and enforcement purposes, holograms, microtext, ultraviolet security features, laser engraving and a chip with a higher level of encryption.
The improvements are expected to enhance identity verification capabilities while reducing the risk of forgery and misuse of identification documents.
The rollout of the New Structure MyKad is expected to be launched by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim on a date to be announced.
As an initial step, NRD announced that the new card will be issued at its headquarters in Putrajaya and 18 selected offices through an appointment system before being expanded nationwide in stages.
The phased approach is intended to ensure a smooth implementation process and avoid congestion at service counters.
NRD also advised the public not to rush to replace their MyKad, as the rollout will be carried out gradually and will not involve simultaneous replacement of all cards.
Priority will be given to applicants who make appointments through the appointment system, as well as holders of Plastic Identity Cards (KPP), High-Quality Identity Cards (KPT) and Version 1 MyKad.
"Holders of KPP, KPT and Version 1 MyKad are required to replace their cards with the New Structure MyKad in stages within three years," the department said.
NRD assured that existing identity cards remain valid for all official transactions, including the redemption of subsidies and government assistance, and that the security of cardholders' personal data will continue to be safeguarded.
For further information, the public may visit NRD's official website at www.jpn.gov.my or its official social media channels.
The introduction of the New Structure MyKad marks the latest chapter in the evolution of Malaysia's national identification system, which began in 1948 with paper-based identity cards in Peninsular Malaysia before being extended to Sabah and Sarawak.
The system later evolved to plastic identity cards in the 1960s to improve durability, followed by the introduction of high-quality identity cards in the 1980s with enhanced security features.
Malaysia made history in 2001 with the launch of the MyKad, recognised as one of the world's earliest multipurpose smart cards.
The technology was further enhanced in 2012 with a more advanced security chip, while the New Structure MyKad represents the latest step in the continuing evolution of the country's identity system.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Berkshire Hathaway to buy US homebuilder Taylor Morrison for US$6.8bil
Article:
NEW YORK: Berkshire Hathaway agreed on Sunday to buy Taylor Morrison Home Corp for US$6.8 billion in cash, expanding the conglomerate's housing business.
The transaction is Berkshire's first multi-billion-dollar acquisition since Greg Abel became chief executive at the start of 2026, replacing Warren Buffett, who remains chairman.
It also lets Berkshire spend some of its cash hoard, which ended March at US$380.2 billion.
Abel also oversees most of Berkshire's common stock portfolio, led by Apple and including a growing stake in Alphabet.
Berkshire agreed to pay US$72.50 per share for Taylor Morrison, a 24 per cent premium over the Scottsdale, Arizona-based company's closing stock price of US$58.50 on Friday.
The companies said the takeover has an enterprise value of US$8.5 billion.
Taylor Morrison posted net income of US$782.5 million on revenue of US$8.12 billion in 2025.
Berkshire, based in Omaha, Nebraska, has long owned and invested in the housing industry.
It acquired Clayton Homes in 2003.
Other Berkshire subsidiaries include building products companies such as Acme Brick, Benjamin Moore and Johns Manville, and one of the largest US residential real estate brokerages.
Berkshire's stock portfolio included stakes in Lennar and NVR at the end of March.
BERKSHIRE ENVISIONS 'COMBINED PLATFORM' FOR HOMEBUILDING
In a statement, Abel called Taylor Morrison a "best-in-class national homebuilder", and said Berkshire expects to "unify our site-built homebuilding operations into a combined platform" to make it easier for Americans to buy homes.
Taylor Morrison operates in 12 US states under the Taylor Morrison, Esplanade and Yardly brands, including entry-level and "resort lifestyle" housing.
It ranks No. 6 among Builder magazine's top 100 homebuilders. Taylor Morrison also provides mortgage financing, as do some Clayton units.
Sheryl Palmer, Taylor Morrison's chief executive, called Berkshire's long-term focus "uniquely well-suited to the multi-year investment cycle of homebuilding", and said the purchase "will allow us to scale the Taylor Morrison platform in ways that would not be possible as a standalone company".
The transaction is expected to close in the second half of 2026 pending shareholder and regulatory approvals, and Palmer will remain chief executive.
Taylor Morrison and Berkshire did not immediately respond to requests for additional comment.
In January, Berkshire paid US$9.5 billion in cash for Occidental Petroleum's chemicals business. That transaction was announced last October.
Goldman Sachs, Moelis & Company and the law firms Simpson Thacher & Bartlett and Mayer Brown advised Taylor Morrison on the transaction, the company said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Prison riot in Mexico leaves seven inmates dead
Article:
MEXICO CITY: Rival gangs clashed in a prison in a northwestern Mexican state that is home to a powerful drug cartel, leaving seven inmates dead, officials said Sunday.
The fighting broke out overnight Saturday into Sunday in a prison in Culiacan, the capital of Sinaloa state. The prison houses many inmates from the cartel of the same name.
An internecine war between the rival factions has left more than 1,000 people dead since 2024.
The Sinaloa state security ministry said the riot was brought under control but family visits have been suspended.
The rival factions are loyal to one or the other of the cartel's two co-founders: Joaquin "El Chapo" Guzman and Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada. Both are imprisoned in the United States.
Authorities did not say what they think caused the burst of violence.
After the riot, state and federal police began an exhaustive search of the prison, the ministry said.
Mexican prisons are famously overcrowded and often see fighting between rival gangs.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dollar steadies as markets await signals on Iran war, central banks
Article:
TOKYO: The US dollar held steady on Monday after a weekly loss as markets awaited the results of peace talks in the Middle East and signals on the timing of central bank rate hikes.
The dollar index edged lower last week on hopes for a deal between the United States and Iran to open the Strait of Hormuz shipping lane for oil.
Oil jumped in early trade after Israel ordered troops to move further into Lebanon in the battle with Iranian-backed Hezbollah.
US President Donald Trump said on Friday he would soon decide on a proposed deal to extend the Iran ceasefire.
US jobs data later in the week will be in focus as Federal Reserve officials signal that the US central bank may need to raise rates if the war accelerates already-high inflation.
"USD will be heavily influenced by developments in the US-Iran war and the US non-farm payrolls report for May," said Joseph Capurso, head of FX at Commonwealth Bank of Australia.
"Once the Strait is reopened, over time the oil price will fade and interest rates will return as a greater influence on the USD," he added in a note.
The dollar index of the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, was flat at 99.00, after last week's drop of 0.40 per cent. The euro fell 0.08 per cent to US$1.165.
The yen weakened 0.08 per cent to 159.41 per dollar. Sterling slipped 0.07 per cent to US$1.3449.
A proposed deal would extend the US-Iran ceasefire by 60 days and allow traffic to resume through the waterway, in normal times a conduit for a fifth of global shipments of crude oil and LNG, while negotiators work through contentious issues.
A senior Iranian source told Reuters an agreement was close but had not yet been approved.
US nonfarm payrolls data due on June 5 are expected to show an unemployment rate of 4.30 per cent and an increase of 85,000 jobs, according to a Reuters poll as of Friday.
Financial markets are betting the Fed's next move will be to raise its key rate from the current range of 3.50 per cent to 3.75 per cent, probably by year-end. Officials had been eyeing a rate cut before the start of the Iran war.
The European Central Bank should raise rates this month even if a US-Iran peace deal is reached, Isabel Schnabel, an ECB board member, told Reuters last week. She is set to speak in South Korea on Monday.
A speech by Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda on Wednesday is highly anticipated for signals on whether the central bank will proceed with a rate increase the following week.
While there is no consensus yet within the BOJ on the decision, a pause in the central bank's taper of government bond purchases is increasingly seen as a preferred option, said two sources familiar with the deliberations.
Japan's finance ministry said on Friday the government spent 11.7 trillion yen (US$73.40 billion) intervening in currency markets over the previous month to support the yen, confirming what traders had widely suspected.
The Australian dollar traded flat at US$0.7181 against the greenback. New Zealand's kiwi fell 0.17 per cent to US$0.5978.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asia stocks count on AI boom to offset Gulf risks
Article:
SYDNEY: Asian share markets firmed on Monday as the boom in all things AI continued to drive demand, offsetting a lack of progress in Gulf peace talks that challenged optimism on a re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz and lifted oil prices.
While negotiators from Washington and Tehran are apparently working to hammer out a deal, President Donald Trump has been notably silent on their progress.
Speaking on Saturday, Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said the US was ready to restart attacks on Iran if a deal could not be reached.
Tensions in the region were not helped by an Israeli push further into Lebanon in the battle against the Iranian-backed Hezbollah militant group.
"While uncertainties remain, the acute risk phase for the global economy should be over if tankers can begin moving again," said Michael Feroli, head of US economics at JPMorgan.
"Still, not everything would return to its pre-conflict place — oil prices are likely to remain elevated for some time, as inventories get rebuilt and the supply infrastructure in the Middle East is repaired."
Indeed, the lack of news nudged Brent up 1.90 per cent to US$92.89 a barrel, while US crude added 2.40 per cent to US$89.46.
Asian share markets remain underpinned by demand for semiconductors and AI-related gear, with Japan's Nikkei up 0.50 per cent, having risen almost five per cent last week to all-time highs.
South Korea rose 1.30 per cent, after surging eight per cent last week, while Taiwan climbed almost six per cent last week.
MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan added 0.20 per cent.
Nvidia boss Jensen Huang kicks off the Computex trade show in Taiwan on Monday with a speech about AI in which he is expected to expound on his company's latest product efforts as well as the island's central role in the industry.
COUNTDOWN TO PAYROLLS
For Europe, EUROSTOXX 50 futures dipped 0.30 per cent, while DAX futures eased 0.20 per cent and FTSE futures lost 0.50 per cent.
S&P 500 futures were up 0.20 per cent, while Nasdaq futures firmed 0.40 per cent after hitting records last week.
Yet the gains have been narrowly based with the AI-linked big 10 companies making up 40 per cent of the S&P 500 and only 21 stocks of the 500 making record highs.
While tech stocks climbed almost 16 per cent in May, consumer discretionary and healthcare managed little more than two per cent, and consumer staples lost more than three per cent.
The inflationary pulse from oil continues to hamper bond markets as US 10-year yields rose three basis points to 4.47 per cent.
Markets imply a 50-50 chance the Federal Reserve will have to hike rates by year-end to prevent rising prices from getting baked into inflationary expectations.
A host of Fed members are set to speak this week, while major data include the ISM survey of manufacturing and the May payrolls report on Friday.
Market forecasts are for a solid rise of 85,000 in employment, keeping the jobless rate steady at 4.30 per cent. Anything stronger would likely see the odds of a hike narrow further.
The market's hawkish outlook has kept the dollar broadly steady, with the Japanese yen and the euro hampered by those regions' reliance on energy imports.
The dollar was a shade firmer on the yen at 159.42, but bulls were wary of risking Japanese intervention on a break of the 160 barrier.
The euro stood at US$1.1645, having spent the past week hemmed in between US$1.1585 and US$1.1661.
In commodity markets, gold was little changed at US$4,535 an ounce, having found little support.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas expected to discuss future with Bersatu tomorrow
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Pas syura council is expected to meet tomorrow, with discussions possibly including the party's future ties with Bersatu, which is said to be facing internal turmoil.
A source said the meeting is expected to be chaired by Pas spiritual leader Datuk Hashim Jasin.
"He will chair the meeting online due to his current health condition.
"Almost all syura council members have confirmed their attendance, including Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang.
"Among the issues expected to be discussed is the relationship with Bersatu, as the Pas central working committee has handed the matter over to the syura council for a decision," he told BH here.
He said all council members had thoroughly reviewed the Pas–Bersatu issue ahead of its presentation tomorrow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Brothers die after Volkswagen skids into tree
Article:
JEMPOL: Two brothers were killed in a road accident at Km 11 of Jalan Bahau–Kemayan here at 8.40pm last night.
Jempol district police chief Superintendent Norhisham Mustapar said the crash involved a Volkswagen car carrying three brothers, who were travelling from Kuala Pilah towards Temerloh, Pahang.
He said the vehicle was driven by a 31-year-old man, with his 25- and 27-year-old siblings as passengers.
"The incident is believed to have occurred when the driver lost control while negotiating a bend before the car skidded to the right shoulder of the opposite lane and crashed into a tree," he said.
As a result of the crash, the driver and the 25-year-old rear passenger, who was a Universiti Kuala Lumpur (UniKL) student, died at the scene from severe injuries.
Norhisham said the front passenger sustained injuries and was taken to Jempol Hospital for treatment.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
Police have urged witnesses to contact investigating officer Inspector Muhammad Fasha Mohd Faisal at 016-3627746 to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King salutes RMAF personnel on 68th anniversary
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, and Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia, conveyed their best wishes to all Royal Malaysian Air Force (RMAF) personnel and veterans in conjunction with its 68th anniversary.
His Majesty, who is also the Commander-in-Chief of the armed forces, expressed his highest appreciation for the commitment of RMAF personnel in defending the sovereignty of the nation's airspace.
"Al-Fatihah to those who have passed on. May they be placed among the faithful and the martyrs," His Majesty said in a Facebook post on Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar's official page today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Call to maintain Asean centrality
Article:
SINGAPORE:  Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin has called on Southeast Asia to remain anchored in Asean centrality and avoid being drawn into choosing sides as  geopolitical tensions  intensify.
Delivering his remarks at the fifth plenary session of the International Institute for Strategic Studies Shangri-La Dialogue, themed "Managing Regional Tensions amid Global Competition", he said Southeast Asia's stability had long been underpinned by Asean's role in fostering dialogue, consensus and mutual respect.
He warned that any weakening of Asean centrality would have far-reaching consequences for the region and the wider international order.
"Southeast Asia faces mounting pressure from growing geopolitical rivalries, geoeconomic fragmentation, technological competition and strategic coercion.
"Asean was founded on cooperation rather than coercion, dominance or transactional politics. It was built upon dialogue, consensus, mutual respect and collective responsibility for regional stability.
"The moment Asean centrality is compromised, regionalism in Southeast Asia will cease to be meaningful and purposeful," he said, adding that such  development would be damaging not only to Southeast Asia, but also to the wider Asia-Pacific region.
He said Asean had demonstrated how middle and smaller powers could cooperate effectively through engagement based on mutual respect.
"Anchored firmly in Asean centrality, external powers engaging with this region must respect Asean's norms, strategic sensitivities and regional mechanisms, not bypass them," he said.
Khaled also called on middle powers to work collectively to restore balance and predictability in global affairs, saying they should not remain passive while larger powers shape or weaken multilateral institutions such as the United Nations.
"We must not stay passive while some powers exert their influence to shape and, at times, paralyse multilateral institutions such as the United Nations," he said.
He added that, collectively, countries in the global majority had both the legitimacy and responsibility not only to defend the rules-based order, but also to strengthen and restore the credibility of the United Nations.
Khaled said the core challenge facing the international system was not merely competition between states, but the gradual erosion of trust in the rules-based global order.
"The deeper issue is the gradual erosion of trust within the rules-based global order and the international system.
"So our responsibility today is not merely to manage tensions. It is to restore credibility, restore consistency and, above all, restore trust," he said.
Khaled said international commitments and agreements must continue to carry weight, warning against selective adherence to international law based on power or influence.
"If trust continues to erode, the world risks drifting from a rules-based order to a lawless, anarchic environment," he said, adding that history had shown the consequences of such  trajectory.
Khaled, who led the Malaysian delegation to the dialogue, said key outcomes included strengthened defence ties with more than 10 partner countries, participation in underwater infrastructure security initiatives and discussions on defence industry cooperation.
Throughout the forum, Khaled held bilateral meetings with counterparts from the United States, France, Italy, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Norway, the Czech Republic, Qatar, Singapore and Thailand.
Malaysia also held discussions with Embraer Defence and Security on potential cooperation in capability development, technology transfer and strengthening the domestic defence ecosystem.
A key outcome was Malaysia's participation in the launch of the Guiding Principles for Underwater Infrastructure Defence Exchanges (GUIDE) with 16 countries, reflecting its commitment to protecting critical subsea infrastructure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Social media age verification begins today
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia today began enforcing age verification requirements for the registration and opening of social media accounts as a proactive measure to protect children under the age of 16 from cyber threats.
The move is being implemented in line with the enforcement of the Child Protection Code (CPC) and Risk Mitigation Code (RMC) which also began today under the Online Safety Act 2025 (ONSA) by the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC).
Under the new regulations, individuals under the age of 16 are no longer allowed to open any social media accounts, while those who wish to register must submit official identification documents such as MyKad, passport or MyDigital ID to verify their age.
The requirement is not limited to new registrations, but existing account holders are also required to verify their identities to ensure they remain eligible to access the platforms according to the set criteria.
The implementation puts Malaysia in line with global trends, such as Australia, in restricting access to social media to protect young users, in addition to following Indonesia which enforced similar regulations on March 28.
However, Malaysia's approach has its own advantages because it leverages specific legislation on MyKad and the MyDigital ID system as additional security elements to ensure more authentic and secure identity verification.
The MCMC, in an earlier statement, said the enforcement of both codes aims to strengthen the child protection ecosystem as well as requiring digital platform providers to act more proactively in dealing with harmful content.
The two codes were developed through in-depth engagement sessions and public consultations with various stakeholders, including industry players and civil society organisations, since February.
Under the CPC, platform providers are required to adopt the safety by design principle, including limiting functions that risk exposing children to the threat of cyber exploitation, online grooming by digital predators and inappropriate content.
The RMC, meanwhile, requires service providers to implement comprehensive risk assessments, stricter content governance measures and a labeling mechanism for manipulated content to curb the threat of fraud and fake news.
Service providers who fail to comply with the requirements under the RMC may face stern action, including fines or financial penalties of up to RM10 million.
As part of efforts to curb online fraud, paid advertisements for goods or services will only be allowed if uploaded by advertisers or users whose identities have been verified based on official government documents.
The MCMC also requires platform providers to adjust their algorithms, including content recommendation systems, to reduce the risk of users being exposed to harmful material such as child sexual abuse material (CSAM), pornographic content and financial scams.
According to the commission, a reasonable implementation period will be given to all service providers to ensure that the process of integrating the age verification system runs smoothly without compromising user privacy.
ONSA, fully enforced on Jan 1, 2026, aims to ensure digital platform providers shoulder greater responsibility for detecting, preventing, and responding more swiftly to harmful content in cyberspace.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Body in mattress: Victim believed to be suspect's acquaintance
Article:
MELAKA: A decomposed body found wrapped in a mattress at a shop lot in Pokok Mangga here on Thursday is believed to be an acquaintance of the suspect arrested by police.
State police chief Datuk Dzulkhairi Mukhtar said the finding resulted from investigations into the suspect, who had been detained within 24 hours of the body's discovery, Kosmo reported today.
He said the motive for the murder is believed to have stemmed from a fight due to a misunderstanding and accusations involving the suspect's family, while both of them were under the influence of drugs.
"However, the identity of the victim has yet to be fully confirmed and is subject to a deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) comparison process with family members.
"The suspect has been remanded for seven days from May 29 until June 4 to assist in the investigations," he said on Sunday.
The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
It was reported that a body was found in a
severely decomposed state
, wrapped in a mattress with both hands tied, at a shop lot in Pokok Mangga, believed to be linked to a murder case.
Following the incident, police arrested an unemployed man in his 40s to assist in investigations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 9 million workers covered 24/7: Socso's Lindung 24 starts today
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: More than nine million contributors under the Employees' Social Security Act are now automatically covered against non-workplace accidents under the Lindung 24 scheme, effective today.
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan said the initiative expands Malaysia's social security safety net, extending protection beyond working hours, including during public holidays and rest days.
He said the scheme, under the Social Security Organisation (Socso) would be automatically applied to all existing formal-sector employees without requiring fresh registration by employers or workers.
"Those employed and reporting for duty from today (June 1) onwards must be registered by their employers in accordance with existing procedures through the Perkeso system," he said in a statement today.
Ramanan said contributions for the Lindung 24 scheme would be fully borne by employees and implemented in stages, subject to the contribution salary ceiling of RM6,000.
He said the initial contribution rate has been set at 0.75 per cent of monthly wages, with employers responsible for managing salary deductions and remitting monthly payments to Socso.
"Contributions must be paid continuously to ensure uninterrupted protection coverage, while also facilitating compliance among employers and employees," he said.
[[nid:1452576]]
Ramanan added under the scheme, Socso provides eight benefits aimed at safeguarding contributors' welfare and livelihood, including medical benefit, temporary disablement benefit, permanent disablement benefit and dependants' benefit. .
"The scheme also covers constant attendance allowance, funeral benefits, physical rehabilitation, vocational rehabilitation and Return To Work (RTW) programmes, as well as education benefits to support beneficiaries' future."
Ramanan said the expanded protection is in line with recommendations by the International Labour Organisation and international best practices aimed at strengthening workers' social security networks.
"Socso's latest initiative has made Malaysia's social protection framework more comprehensive, dynamic and responsive to current labour market realities, while supporting the Malaysia Madani agenda in safeguarding workers' welfare and wellbeing."
Applications for benefits can be submitted online via the Lindung Faedah portal at lindungfaedah.perkeso.gov.my or at the nearest Perkeso office.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AI to reshape, not replace jobs, with wage pressure seen as bigger risk
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Artificial intelligence (AI) is expected to reshape employment in Malaysia over the long term rather than broadly displace jobs, with economists saying the impact is more likely to fall on tasks and wages than full occupations.
A widely cited projection that a large share of jobs could be at risk of automation has since been revised, with economists highlighting methodological limitations and a shift in focus from entire occupations to individual tasks.
As AI has rapidly integrated into the global economy, newer economic thinking suggests the impact is more nuanced than full-scale job replacement, said economist Dr Geoffrey Williams.
He told the Business Times the newer framework suggests AI will alter specific tasks within jobs rather than eliminate whole occupations.
"Technology-resistant sectors characterised by high human contact, emotional intelligence and complex personal interaction continue to be a substantial component of employment," he said.
He added that wages are not closely tied to productivity. "They are determined by supply and demand in the labour market, the bargaining power of employees and regulations such as the minimum wage."
To prevent prolonged wage suppression, you need to improve worker bargaining power through enforcement of laws or unions. You can also extend the progressive wage by removing complexities and turning it into a reverse income tax. Below a threshold, say RM3,100, living wage workers would receive a tax credit. Above the threshold, they pay tax as normal," he said.
Dr Williams said more recent estimates suggest that as few as 9.0 per cent of jobs could be fully automated, although between 28 per cent and 48 per cent of jobs may see significant portions of their tasks impacted by AI-driven technologies, including automation and digitalisation.
He said "net job creation" should be measured as the difference between jobs created and those made redundant, while stressing that job "quality" is subjective and can include pay, working hours, workload, flexibility, career progression and overall job satisfaction.
On the timing of productivity gains from general-purpose technologies such as AI, he said the lag between adoption and measurable impact varies widely across sectors.
"For micro-enterprises, the lag can be as short as one to three months," he said. "For large organisations, it is typically one to three years. For the broader economy, it can take five to 15 years. For AI this could be quicker, but we have no definitive data yet," he said.
On the so-called "Solow paradox"—the observation that rapid technological advancement does not always translate into immediate productivity gains—Dr Williams said measurement challenges remain.
He said the phenomenon is widely cited, but current statistical tools are still not fully able to capture AI-driven productivity improvements in real time.
He also noted that in economies with a relatively low compensation of employees (CE) ratio, such as Malaysia's 32 per cent to 33 per cent of gross domestic product, AI adoption could further reduce labour's share of income.
"The implication is that when productivity gains eventually materialise, they are likely to accrue disproportionately to employers unless labour institutions are strengthened," he said.
Dr Williams said structural underemployment would persist in sectors such as healthcare, education and personalised services, where human interaction remains difficult to automate.
He added that AI is expected to both replace some jobs and transform many others through task automation, while also creating new forms of employment.
"The effect could be wage compression," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Netanyahu pledges expanded Lebanon offensive after Beaufort seizure
Article:
QLAYAA, Lebanon: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu vowed to push deeper into Lebanon after his military took over the medieval castle of Beaufort on Sunday, calling it a "dramatic shift" in the campaign against Hizbollah.
The Iran-backed group, meanwhile, said it targeted Israeli forces near the fortress as well as army positions and infrastructure in Shlomi and Nahariya in northern Israel, while air raid sirens blared in the Acre area.
Israel pursued its strikes on Lebanon, with eight people were killed in a strike on Deir Zahrani in southern Lebanon on Sunday including three women, according to the Lebanese health ministry.
As fighting escalated in Lebanon, diplomatic sources told AFP that the United Nations Security Council would hold an emergency meeting Monday over Israel's expansion of its offensive in the country.
The meeting was requested by France, whose President Emmanuel Macron said "nothing justifies the major escalation under way in south Lebanon", calling for an end to fighting "for good."
Lebanon was dragged into the Middle East war on March 2 when Hizbollah fired rockets towards Israel in retaliation for the US-Israeli killing of Iran's supreme leader.
A truce to halt the fighting between Israel and Hizbollah began on April 17, but has never been observed. Both sides accuse each other daily of violating the ceasefire and justify their attacks by the other's alleged breaches.
Military delegations from Lebanon and Israel held security talks in Washington on Friday, with more US-brokered negotiations planned next week.
Israeli forces used the Beaufort castle, also known as Qalaat al-Chakif, as a base during their previous two-decade occupation of southern Lebanon that ended in 2000.
In a video statement released after the military took Beaufort, Netanyahu said "we have returned united, determined and stronger than ever."
"Now my directive is to deepen and expand our hold in places that were under Hizbollah's control. The capture of Beaufort is a dramatic stage and a dramatic shift in the policy we are leading."
Shelling was audible and smoke rose from the surrounding area as AFP saw the Israeli flag above the castle.
Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz said troops had captured the historic strongpoint, which commands sweeping views of south Lebanon, as they expanded their ground operations.
"Forty-four years after the heroic Battle of Beaufort, and on this day commemorating the soldiers who fell in the First Lebanon War (1982), our troops have returned to the summit of Beaufort and once again raised the Israeli flag there," Katz said.
- 'WE WILL RETURN' -
In a shelter for the displaced in Sidon, southern Lebanon's largest city, Zeinab Fakih, from Nabatieh, told AFP "we are afraid."
"It is impossible for us to return to our home, because the city is in great destruction," she said, adding that the arrival of Israeli forces at the castle was "tragic."
Issa Tfaily, also displaced from Nabatieh, said: "We will return... if not today, then tomorrow, as long as there is resistance."
The push to Beaufort came as the Israeli military issued a sweeping evacuation order to areas south of the Zahrani River, north of the Litani and around 40km from the border.
It later said it had targeted "dozens of Hizbollah infrastructure sites since this morning" in the Tyre area and other parts of southern Lebanon, while Lebanon's state-run National News Agency reported a series of strikes on the area.
An Israeli strike near a hospital in Tyre wounded 13 staffers, the Lebanese health ministry said.
An AFP correspondent in the city saw levelled buildings and rescuers operating at the scene.
Another correspondent said the strikes were the strongest on the city since the war started.
A few thousand people remain in Tyre's small old city, spared from Israeli evacuation warnings, some sleeping in their cars.
In Sidon, an AFP photographer saw civil defence teams from the Tyre region reach the city after Israel's military called them to evacuate.
Ali Safieddine, civil defence head in Tyre city, said they have "temporarily relocated to Sidon."
The Israeli army said a Hizbollah explosive drone killed one of its soldiers Saturday, bringing to 25 the number of Israeli military deaths in Lebanon since early March.
It added that "since the start of the ceasefire, 900 Hizbollah fighters have been eliminated."
Lebanon's health ministry says Israeli attacks have killed more than 3,412 people since early March.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US tariff cut offers Karex RM8mil boost, but losses persist: CIMB Securities
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tariff relief from lower US import duties could provide a near-term boost to Karex Bhd, with the condom maker potentially receiving refunds of about US$2 million (RM8 million) in the fourth quarter after tariff rates were cut to 10 per cent from 19 per cent, effective March 2026.
However, CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd said the potential upside was not included in its forecasts, noting that Karex is still expected to remain loss-making in the fourth quarter of financial year 2026 (Q4 FY26) as foreign exchange headwinds and elevated costs continue to weigh on margins.
US president Donald Trump imposed a temporary 10 per cent import duty in February, days after the Supreme Court struck down his earlier sweeping tariffs. The US trade court on May 7 ruled against the latest tariff, but the US Courts of Appeals issued a stay after the government appealed.
In a research note, CIMB Securities said the worst may be over for Karex following the third quarter of FY26, with sales volumes expected to recover sequentially on improved order fulfilment and tighter cost controls.
To offset cost pressures, Karex has started implementing gradual selling price increases of between 20 per cent and 25 per cent, although CIMB Securities said customer acceptance will be key.
CIMB Securties said that pricing power is supported by a tighter industry supply backdrop following capacity rationalisation and margin pressure that has led to tender withdrawals.
While the impact of higher selling prices is expected to be more visible from Q1 FY27 onwards, CIMB Securities said the reset is structurally positive as it re-bases industry pricing.
The firm downgraded the group's FY26 forecast to a core net loss of RM3.0 million, from a previous core net profit estimate of RM5.9 million, to reflect higher cost pressures, while leaving FY27 to FY28 earnings forecasts unchanged.
Nevertheless, the firm maintained a "Buy" call on the group, with an unchanged target price of 70 sen.
CIMB Securities said that despite near-term headwinds, investors should take a longer-term view supported by Karex's patented nitrile technology and its exclusive OEM supply relationship with a major global condom brand, which underpins multi-year volume visibility beyond FY26.
Karex posted a higher-than-expected Q3 core net loss of RM5.1 million, compared with a core net profit of RM6.0 million a year ago, as revenue declined 13.9 per cent quarter-on-quarter.
For the nine-month period (9M FY26), the group recorded a core net loss of RM0.7 million, below both CIMB Securities' and consensus' FY26 forecasts of RM5.9 million and RM5.4 million, respectively.
Revenue fell 4.3 per cent year-on-year (YoY) to RM361 million, driven by weaker contributions from the sexual wellness segment and the medical segment.
CIMB Securities said the decline was due to a stronger ringgit against the US dollar, averaging RM4.1, lower tender volumes, and softer overall demand despite higher synthetic condom sales.
Gross profit margin fell 1.8 percentage points YoY, while higher costs and about US$2 million (RM11.1 million) in US tariffs weighed on before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (Ebitda) margins.
Quarterly losses widened to RM5.1 million in Q3, driven by a stronger ringgit, weaker lubricant sales due to bottle shortages and delays, and lower synthetic condom output during product refinement and validation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Colombians elect president amid rising drug violence
Article:
BOGOTÁ: Polls started to close Sunday in Colombia's presidential election, a high-stakes vote that could bring further peace talks with armed groups or a hard-right military crackdown.
Pre-election polls showed left-wing senator Ivan Cepeda narrowly ahead but facing a strong challenge from hard-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella.
The vote is, in part, a referendum on outgoing President Gustavo Petro and his "total peace" policy of negotiating with guerrillas and other drug trafficking groups.
A decade after a landmark peace accords, pockets of Colombia are still ruled by dissident armed groups that dominate world cocaine production.
Critics say Petro's strategy has given these gangs free rein, causing surging violence and record drug exports.
The presidential campaign was marred by car bombs, drone attacks, and the assassination of one leading presidential candidate, Miguel Uribe.
"This government really strengthened armed groups by being so soft," said Catalina Devia, a 42-year-old advertising executive and mother of two who voted for De la Espriella.
"Many Colombians are thinking about emigrating," she said.
Rightwing frontrunner De la Espriella, 47, is a pro-Trump outsider who calls himself "The Tiger."
He has campaigned behind bullet-proof glass and vowed to confront armed groups in the air, on land and at sea – mimicking rhetoric that has swept the right to power across Latin America.
"We are going to win in the first round," De la Espriella said after casting his ballot.
But first he must overcome Cepeda, 63, the son of a leftist senator killed by right-wing paramilitaries.
Cepeda is backed by President Petro, who is constitutionally barred from re-election, and draws support from voters who credit the government with helping the poor.
They have praised Petro for raising the minimum wage, boosting education spending and transferring land to the poor.
"I think we've done quite a lot in terms of education...protecting the environment, social justice, and defending human rights," said Pedro Barragan, a 52-year-old teacher voting in central Bogota.
- 'WHAT I FEAR' -
Third-placed conservative Senator Paloma Valencia, an ally of former president Alvaro Uribe, also backs a militarised approach while seeking to attract centrist voters.
"We are very calm, very happy, trusting in God and in the Colombian people," she told AFP outside a polling station.
Some voters expressed unease with the stark and highly polarised choice presented by leading candidates.
"My vote is not guided by what I want, but what I fear the most," said Julian, a 37-year-old project manager. "I'm going to vote for the less worse candidate."
In conflict-hit regions, voters voiced anxiety over security but also demanded social investment.
"What do I expect from the new government? That it take Indigenous communities into account," said Yorelis Polanco, a member of the Wayuu community near the Venezuelan border.
Despite worsening violence in rebel-held areas, election day itself passed calmly.
Authorities deployed more than 400,000 police and soldiers nationwide to secure polling stations.
Whoever replaces Petro will have to reckon with an alphabet soup of criminal groups engaging in drug trafficking, illegal mining and extortion. -AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesia issues early warning for coastal flooding in Bali
Article:
JAKARTA: Indonesia's meteorological authority on Sunday issued an early warning for potential coastal flooding across several areas of Bali, the country's famed resort island, reported Xinhua.
Meteorology, Climatology, and Geophysics Agency Region III head Cahyo Nugroho said the full moon phenomenon on Sunday is expected to trigger a significant rise in sea levels.
The potential for flooding is projected to persist through June 5.
Based on water-level monitoring and tidal forecasts, the warning covers the southern coastline of the island, including several tourist hubs.
Authorities warned that the higher-than-normal tides could disrupt port operations and impact coastal settlements, salt ponds, and inland fisheries.
The public has been urged to remain vigilant and take precautionary measures against the potential impacts of the high tides over the coming days.  – Bernama-
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia unlikely to lose edge despite Indonesia FTA head start [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Indonesia's move to finalise a free trade agreement (FTA) with the European Union (EU) could sharpen price competition for Malaysian palm oil exports, but analysts say Malaysia is unlikely to lose its competitive edge in the global market.
Indonesia, the world's largest palm oil exporter,  concluded negotiations with the EU on Sept 23 last year. The deal is  undergoing legal review and ratification, with entry into force targeted for Jan 1 next year.
In contrast, Malaysia has made slower progress in its FTA talks with the EU, completing only the third round of negotiations in February this year.
The fourth round  resumes this month.
Indonesia's palm oil exports  were estimated at 23.6 million tonnes in 2025, generating  US$24.4 billion.
Malaysia, the second-largest exporter, shipped  17.3 million tonnes of palm oil last year.
While smaller in volume compared with Indonesia, Malaysia maintains a strong position in higher-value segments, particularly certified and refined palm oil products.
Export earnings were estimated at about RM120 billion.
STRATEGIC MEASURES
Consulting firm ESG in Malaysia executive director Dr Harald Sippel told Business Times: "While there is a first-mover advantage, the situation is more nuanced than it appears at first glance."
Malaysia still has room to respond with its own strategic countermeasures, he added.
He said Indonesia's concluded Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement gives it an early platform in working with the EU on sustainability and compliance requirements under the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), which now plays a bigger role than tariffs in determining palm oil market access.
"That said, Malaysia has been pursuing what I would describe as a parallel regulatory diplomacy track that the FTA timeline debate almost entirely misses.
"In September 2025, ahead of its own FTA conclusion, Malaysia secured recognition of its Malaysian Sustainable Palm Oil (MSPO) certification in a joint statement with the EU.
"This is significant because EUDR compliance hinges not just on tariffs but on whether a country's domestic certification scheme is recognised as credible.
"Malaysia moved on that front independently of its FTA timeline."
Sippel said Malaysia also concluded an economic partnership agreement with the European Free Trade Association (EFTA) states in June 2025, which includes a joint statement on palm oil.
He added that while the EFTA deal is not equivalent to an EU FTA, it helps establish frameworks and precedents that could support future FTA negotiations with the union.
"Indonesia has moved first on the FTA. But Malaysia is not standing still, and the competitive picture is more textured than a simple race narrative suggests."
Fitch Solutions' unit BMI commodities analyst Bin Hui Ong said an earlier conclusion of an EU FTA by Indonesia could intensify price competition for Malaysian palm oil exports, particularly if tariff reductions improve Indonesia's relative price competitiveness.
The pressure could be further amplified by expectations of a stronger ringgit and a relatively weaker Indonesian rupiah over 2026.
"However, we believe Malaysia will retain a degree of competitive resilience, supported by its strong progress in sustainability certification.
"With close to 90 per cent of acreage certified under the MSPO scheme (as of June 2025), recognised by the EU as compliant with EUDR requirements, we believe Malaysia is well positioned to meet evolving regulatory standards and maintain market access, differentiating its exports beyond price considerations."
REGULATORY GAP OUTWEIGHS TARIFF RISKS
On whether Malaysia could face weaker exports if its FTA is concluded later, Sippel said a period of relatively weaker export performance compared with Indonesia is possible, but should not be overstated.
He added that Malaysia's palm oil exports will continue to be governed by  World Trade Organisation-bound tariff arrangements in the meantime, which remain non-prohibitive.
"The more pressing risk is not tariff disadvantage but regulatory disadvantage, specifically, whether Malaysian exporters are sufficiently prepared to meet EUDR requirements."
He added that  Indonesia, through its CEPA sustainability cooperation framework, is  working with the EU to build compliance capacity.
He said the EU regulatory environment for palm oil is not static, and even with an FTA in place, exporters would still need to navigate an evolving set of requirements.
These include compliance with the EUDR, the Renewable Energy Directive's gradual phase-out of palm oil in biofuels, the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism and corporate sustainability due diligence obligations.
"We describe this as a 'moving regulatory frontier', and it means that export readiness is a continuous process, not a one-time compliance exercise."
He added that Malaysian companies that prepare early on traceability, certification and EU rules will be better positioned than those that wait for the FTA.
Sippel said while an earlier conclusion of the FTA would speed up market access, it would not replace the need for  preparation.
He said the FTA establishes formal bilateral frameworks through which the EU and its Asean partners can negotiate regulatory alignment, including EUDR implementation, certification recognition and traceability cooperation.
"That is where the real long-term value lies, for  Indonesia and Malaysia.
"The FTA is less a market-opening instrument for palm oil specifically and more a platform for the kind of regulatory diplomacy that will determine future  EU market access conditions."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oversupply darkens glove sector outlook [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Glove average selling prices (ASPs) are expected to remain supported this year, although the sector outlook remains challenging amid persistent oversupply.
CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd expects the global glove industry to remain oversupplied over the next two years, with annual production capacity projected to exceed 500 billion pieces in financial years 2026 and 2027, significantly above estimated demand of around 400 billion pieces.
It  said while ASPs are likely to stay supportive in the fourth quarter of financial year 2026, it forecasts a decline of at least US$2 per 1,000 pieces from the first quarter of financial year 2027.
Against this backdrop, it said Supermax Corp Bhd could remain in the red in financial year 2027 if ASPs decline from current levels.
"We believe Supermax could remain loss-making in financial year 2027, given its high-cost operating structure and the slower-than-expected ramp-up of its US operations," it said in a note yesterday.
CIMB Securities maintained a neutral stance on recent United States tariff developments.
On May 12, a US federal appeals court temporarily stayed a ruling by the US Court of International Trade that  declared President Donald Trump's 10 per cent global tariffs, imposed under Section 122, unlawful.
The tariffs, introduced on Feb 10, remain in effect.
"Malaysia has since established a dominant position in the US glove market, capturing a 65 per cent to 70 per cent market share (versus China's 1.0 per cent to 2.0 per cent), while Chinese exports are subject to tariffs of 110 per cent (comprising 100 per cent Section 301 tariffs and an additional 10 per cent global tariffs)."
Supermax said recently that it plans to invest US$50 million in a new glove manufacturing facility in Parana, Brazil, which will be funded through internal cash and developed in two phases by its associate company Supermax Brasil Importadora S/A.
It will have an annual installed capacity of 2.4 billion gloves and manufacture both latex and nitrile examination gloves.
CIMB Securities said Supermax expects a turnaround in the second half of 2026, supported by stronger contributions from the plant after validation is completed by this month, as well as higher ASPs amid rising input costs.
It added that  it has not factored the Brazilian project into its forecasts due to the absence of a clear implementation timeline.
"However, we expect Supermax to remain loss-making in financial year 2027,  as its US operations face higher operating costs compared with Malaysian peers," the firm said in a note.
CIMB Securities maintained its  2026 core net loss forecast but lowered financial years 2027 and 2028 projections to RM59 million and RM69 million, respectively, from RM102 million and RM94 million previously.
It  raised its target price to 28 sen from 26 sen based on an unchanged 0.2 times 2027 price-to-book multiple.
Meanwhile, the firm raised its 2026 core net profit forecast for Kossan Rubber Industries Bhd by 9.0 per cent to reflect higher ASP and cost assumptions. However, it trimmed 2027 and 2028 earnings forecast by 25 per cent and 18 per cent, respectively.
"Following our earnings adjustments, we have raised our target price to RM1.26 (from RM1.15)," CIMB Securities said.
"We think Kossan's exposure to non-generic products (17 per cent of group revenue last year) should offer  earnings resilience.
"Its  balance sheet profile, with total net cash of RM1.6 billion up to  March (54 per cent of its market cap, the highest among major domestic players), positions it  comfortably to weather near-term industry headwinds."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Beyond oil: The Petronas–Sarawak question is really about Malaysia's future federation
Article:
The real issue is no longer petroleum alone
The public debate surrounding Petronas and Sarawak has often been framed too narrowly. Headlines naturally gravitate towards the immediate dispute: petroleum rights, legal jurisdiction, Petros, the Petroleum Development Act 1974 (PDA), and the constitutional position of Sarawak within the federation.
Yet oil is merely the entry point into a much larger national conversation.
At its core, the issue concerns the evolution of Malaysian federalism itself — how authority, strategic resources, institutional legitimacy and regional aspirations are managed within a modern federation.
This is why the debate has generated such emotional and political intensity. Petroleum in Sarawak is not viewed merely as an economic commodity. It is intertwined with historical memory, MA63 expectations, questions of fairness, and perceptions of how power has been exercised since the formation of Malaysia in 1963.
The challenge therefore is not simply about who controls oil.
The deeper question is whether Malaysia can modernise its federal compact before institutional ambiguity evolves into a strategic and economic liability.
That distinction matters greatly.
Because once framed properly, the Petronas–Sarawak issue ceases to be a simplistic contest between Putrajaya and Kuching. Instead, it becomes a sophisticated statecraft question confronting many federations globally: how does a country preserve national strategic coordination while accommodating stronger regional aspirations?
Federalism, Trust and the Limits of Legal Resolution
There is, of course, a legal dimension to the current dispute.
Petronas seeks legal clarity. Sarawak seeks recognition of historical rights and constitutional understandings. The Federal Court may eventually provide clarification regarding jurisdictional boundaries and legislative interpretation.
But courts can only settle law.
They cannot fully settle political trust.
This is perhaps the most important point often overlooked in public discourse.
If major questions concerning federal-state relations continue ending in litigation rather than institutional negotiation, then the deeper issue is no longer merely legal. It becomes structural.
Malaysia's federal system historically relied on a relatively strong centre. For decades, this helped preserve macro-political stability, coordinate development and build national institutions such as PETRONAS itself. Yet Malaysia today is no longer the Malaysia of the 1970s.
Sarawak and Sabah are now politically more confident, economically more assertive and institutionally more aware of their historical position within the federation.
MA63 is no longer treated merely as an archival document. It has become a living political reference point.
This evolution is not unique to Malaysia.
Canada faces similar tensions with Alberta. The United Kingdom experienced them with Scottish and North Sea oil. Spain continues grappling with Catalonia. Across many federations, strategic resources increasingly intersect with identity, autonomy and political legitimacy.
The lesson from these cases is not that federalism is failing.
The lesson is that modern federations must continuously evolve.
Strong federations are not built on permanent centralisation alone. Nor are they sustained through uncontrolled fragmentation. They survive through negotiated legitimacy, institutional clarity and political trust.
That is the real test now facing Malaysia.
Petronas as a National Institution — and Why Clarity Matters
The debate must also avoid falling into false binaries.
Supporting a stronger role for Sarawak does not require weakening Petronas. Equally, preserving Petronas as a national institution should not mean dismissing legitimate state aspirations.
This is where maturity in governance becomes essential.
Petronas is not simply another corporation. It forms part of Malaysia's broader strategic architecture. It contributes to fiscal stability, energy security, industrial development, technical capability and international credibility.
In a fragmented geopolitical environment, such institutions become even more important.
Today's world is shaped increasingly by sanctions, supply chain disruptions, maritime chokepoints, energy insecurity and geoeconomic competition. Strategic sectors — energy, semiconductors, critical minerals, ports and digital infrastructure — are no longer purely commercial domains. They are instruments of national resilience and strategic leverage.
In such an environment, institutional ambiguity carries economic cost.
Investors may tolerate taxation. They may tolerate strict regulation. What markets struggle to tolerate is uncertainty over authority.
Who regulates? Who issues approvals? Who negotiates with investors? Who guarantees long-term policy consistency? Who ultimately speaks for Malaysia internationally? When answers become unclear, risk premiums rise.
This is why the issue should never be reduced to emotional political confrontation. The more important challenge is whether Malaysia can design a governance framework where Petronas remains nationally strong while Sarawak receives a clearer, more meaningful and more institutionalised role within resource governance.
That is not weakness. That is modern federalism.
Malaysia's Next Federal Compact
Ultimately, the Petronas–Sarawak issue is not merely about hydrocarbons beneath the seabed. It is about the kind of federation Malaysia wishes to become over the next several decades.
A federation that continuously manages tensions through ad hoc compromise and periodic litigation. Or a federation that modernises itself through clearer institutional arrangements, stronger consultation mechanisms, greater transparency and more mature powersharing structures.
The answer cannot be winner-takes-all politics.
A legal victory without political legitimacy may deepen resentment. A political compromise without institutional clarity may only postpone future conflict.
The more sustainable path is a settlement where:
• national coordination remains credible,
• state aspirations are respected,
• investors retain confidence
• Institutional trust is strengthened.
That requires leadership, not merely litigation.
Because the real issue before Malaysia today is not whether Petronsas wins or Sarawak wins.
The real issue is whether Malaysia can demonstrate that strong national institutions and stronger regional dignity can coexist within one coherent federation.
That is the true statecraft challenge of the 21st century.
-----------
* The writer is an adjunct lecturer at Universiti Teknologi Petronas and senior consultant with Global Asia Consulting. The opinions expressed are his own.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas deputy president revives call for Muafakat Nasional with new partners [WATCH]
Article:
KOTA BARU:  Pas deputy president Datuk Seri Tuan Ibrahim Tuan Man has called for the revival of Muafakat Nasional (MN) with a fresh line-up that includes leaders from Umno, Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin's "Reset" camp and other Malay-based  parties.
He said a new-look MN could be what was needed to "unite the ummah".
Tuan Ibrahim, who is also Perikatan Nasional deputy chairman, said he believed that reviving the alliance, which he dubbed "MN 2.0", could help to realise such efforts.
"Personally, I am more in favour of mobilising MN 2.0 in a new form.
"Perhaps this time, it needs to be open to the 'Reset' group (Hamzah's team), Umno and even other parties, such as Pejuang, Berjasa, Iman and Perkasa," the Kubang Kerian member of parliament told the
New Straits Times
.
MN began as a political cooperation pact in 2019 between Umno and Pas to unite Malay voters against the then-ruling Pakatan Harapan coalition.
The alliance was dissolved in 2022 following ideological clashes and after Umno joined the unity government.
Tuan Ibrahim said such a "grand coalition" should not be limited to the peninsula,  and should welcome participation from parties in Sabah  and Sarawak.
However, he said his suggestions were his personal views and that it should be discussed within Pas if the party planned to address the matter.
Tuan Ibrahim was speaking before  the party's syura council meeting today, which would be chaired by Pas spiritual leader Datuk Hashim Jasin and attended by senior leaders, including party president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang.
Sources said among  the agenda were the status of Pas' future with its PN partner Bersatu, as well as its position within the opposition alliance.
It is understood that the Pas central committee, after deliberating the issue, had left it to the syura council to decide  on the matter.
The syura council is the party's highest-ranking body, determining its policies, direction and matters pertaining to its constitution.
Meanwhile, Tuan Ibrahim was also asked to comment on Pas information chief Ahmad Fadhli Shaari's suggestion that the party leave PN and go solo in future elections.
Tuan Ibrahim said each party should be free to determine its own future if mutual understanding no longer existed and leaders continued to attack one another publicly.
"Personally, I support this and believe that if there is no understanding and parties continue attacking one another, then every party is free to make decisions regarding its own future," he said.
Ties between the two PN partners came under the spotlight recently after Hadi questioned Bersatu's recent leadership purges and internal decisions, accusing the party of weakening PN, violating coalition trust and prompting a review of their political cooperation ahead of the 16th General Election.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Move on from missile deal and find replacement' - Analysts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Malaysia must pivot immediately to an alternative to the Naval Strike Missile (NSM) system as there is no likelihood that Norway will change its mind on the cancellation of export permits.
Defence analyst Zaki Salleh drew parallels between what Malaysia is now experiencing to the United States' Pressler Amendment in 1990 which halted military aid to Pakistan.
As such, he said the government should formally close the NSM procurement chapter and pursue legal action to recover its losses.
He said  Norway was unlikely to reinstate the export licence for the NSM procurement despite apologising to Malaysia in a  meeting at the International Institute for Strategic Studies Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore.
"From a strategic standpoint, pursuing a solution that could be implemented immediately would be more beneficial than waiting for a reversal that may never materialise," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Zaki said the US embargo on Pakistan under the Pressler Amendment  was a historical cautionary example of the missile impasse.
The US embargo on Pakistan, mandated by the Pressler Amendment to the Foreign Assistance Act, halted all US military and economic assistance.
It was triggered when  president George H.W. Bush refused to certify that Pakistan did not possess a nuclear explosive device, a requirement introduced by Senator Larry Pressler in 1985.
"Although Pakistan eventually received financial compensation after nine years, the 28 F-16 fighter aircraft it had paid for were never delivered.
"That case demonstrates that compensation or diplomatic assurances do not necessarily replace the military capability lost when it is needed most," he said.
Zaki pointed to Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin's meeting with French Armed Forces and Veterans Affairs Minister Catherine Vautrin on the sidelines of the dialogue as a possible indication that alternative options were being explored.
"While the Defence Ministry has yet to make any official announcement on a replacement for the NSM, the discussions suggested new avenues of cooperation were under consideration.
"According to Khaled, discussions covered defence capability development, defence industry cooperation, maritime security and strategic developments in the Indo-Pacific.
Among the matters discussed were proposals to address the Royal Malaysian Navy's surface-to-surface missile (SSM) requirements following Norway's export cancellation, which has affected the capability development of the littoral combat ship (LCS) programme," Zaki said.
He said  integrating the French-made Exocet MM40 Block 3 anti-ship missile onto the LCS platform would be a proven option, given its operational use by several navies.
Meanwhile, Naif Arab University for Security Sciences  Security Studies Professor Dr Mohd Mizan Aslam proposed that the government come up with an  allocation to  replace the NSM system immediately.
This was   because it could take up to two more years for a suitable SSM to be delivered and fitted to the LCS.
He said the vessels risked being under-equipped without a missile system.
He added that while Norway had apologised over the issue, legal recourse must be pursued.
